file_name: George et al. - 2023 - Hybrid transformation with 1’st order statistics for medical image analysis and classification.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper utilized an Artificial Neural Network (ANN) with a backpropagation method to classify skin cancer types using the ISIC 2018 dataset. The method involved pre-processing dermoscopic images, extracting features via Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT), gradient transforms, and 1st-order statistics, and then training the ANN to achieve classification results (e.g., 85.44% accuracy for DCT sub-bands).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving a proposed hybrid transformation method combining DCT, gradient transforms, and 1st-order statistics for skin cancer classification. It details specific methods (pre-processing, feature extraction, ANN training), uses the ISIC 2018 dataset, and reports new empirical results including accuracy rates (85.44%, 76.07%) and Mean Squared Error (3.52×10^-4), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Hybrid transformation with 1’st order statistics for medical image analysis and classification
brief_summary: This study addresses the early detection of skin cancer by utilizing an artificial neural network (ANN) with a backpropagation method on the ISIC 2018 dataset. The proposed pipeline involves stringent pre-processing to remove hair and normalize images, followed by feature extraction using discrete cosine transform (DCT), gradient transforms, and first-order statistical measures like mean and skewness. The extracted features were used to train an ANN with two hidden layers of 20 nodes each, utilizing a 70/30 train-test split. The hybrid system combining DCT and gradient transforms achieved an accuracy of 85.44% for DCT sub-bands and 76.07% for gradient sub-bands. Additionally, the model demonstrated a mean squared error of 3.52×10^-4 after 1000 epochs, highlighting the effectiveness of combining these transformation methods for accurate skin cancer classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on classifying skin cancer using the ISIC 2018 dataset. The paper mentions distinguishing between 'benign cancers' and 'aberrant and general cancers' (malignant) in the introduction. The classification task involves identifying 'diverse melanomas' and 'various types of skin cancer' as indicated by the 7-class confusion matrix in Table 2 and Table 3, though specific histological labels (e.g., melanoma, BCC) are not explicitly named in the text, only referenced in the context of the ISIC challenge and literature.

lesion_types_studied.role: Target class (implied by dataset and abstract)
lesion_types_studied.details: The abstract states the ANN was utilized to 'discover and index diverse melanomas'. The conclusion mentions research into identifying 'melanoma, a type of skin cancer'. The ISIC 2018 dataset is known for containing melanoma and non-melanoma classes, but the paper does not list the specific 7 classes used in its confusion matrix.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin cancer using the ISIC 2018 dataset. The Artificial Neural Network (ANN) predicts discrete diagnostic categories corresponding to 7 different tumor types (labeled 1-7 in the confusion matrix). The study reports classification accuracy (85.44% for DCT sub-bands, 76.07% for gradient sub-bands) and presents a confusion matrix for the 7-class prediction task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2018 challenge, consisting of dermoscopic images of skin lesions used for classification purposes.

use.training: 70% of the dataset was used for training the Artificial Neural Network (ANN).
use.testing: 30% of the dataset was used for testing the ANN.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Feature extraction from dermoscopic images to characterize image data for classification.
Statistical computation to describe the central tendency of pixel intensity values in the image.
Statistical computation to measure the dispersion of pixel intensity values.
Statistical computation to measure the asymmetry of the pixel intensity distribution.
Statistical computation to measure the tailedness of the pixel intensity distribution.
Statistical computation to measure the difference in intensity between the maximum and minimum values in a sub-region.
Evaluation metric to quantify the difference between predicted and actual values during ANN training.
Evaluation tool to assess classification performance by comparing actual versus predicted tumor types.
Evaluation metric to determine the proportion of correctly classified instances.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Results are presented as accuracy rates, mean squared error (MSE), and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2018.metric: Accuracy, Mean Squared Error (MSE), Detection Ratio
ISIC 2018.notes: The study utilized a 70/30 train-test split. The hybrid method combining DCT, gradient, and 1st-order statistics achieved the highest accuracy (85.44%) and lowest MSE (3.52×10^-4) after 1000 epochs. Table 2 provides detection ratios per tumor class, and Table 3 provides the confusion matrix for the hybrid method.

architecture_details.layers: Input layer, 2 hidden layers, Output layer
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Hybrid method combining Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) on 8x8 sub-bands, Gradient Transform, and 1st-order statistical features (mean, standard deviation, skewness, kurtosis, contrast)

preprocessing_techniques: Noise removal using median filter to eliminate unwanted pixels such as hair from RGB images
Cropping to isolate the cancer region and reduce surrounding white areas
Thinning to minimize the cross-sectional width of cancer strokes by eliminating foreground pixels

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper did not report the use of data augmentation techniques (such as geometric transforms, color transforms, GANs, or synthetic data generation) during training. The study utilized the ISIC 2018 dataset with a 70/30 train-test split and applied pre-processing steps (median filtering, cropping, thinning, normalization, resizing) to the images, but these were not described as augmentation methods to increase dataset size or variability.

conclusions: The hybrid system combining Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT), gradient transforms, and 1st-order statistics achieved an accuracy of 85.44% for DCT sub-bands and 76.07% for gradient sub-bands in skin cancer classification.
The proposed hybrid method significantly reduced the Mean Squared Error (MSE) to 3.52×10^-4 after 1000 epochs, demonstrating high efficiency and accuracy for cancer image categorization.

Combining gradient and DCT approaches produced a more successful method for feature extraction and classification compared to using DCT sub-bands alone, which had lower accuracy and higher error detection.
A key limitation acknowledged is the disparity in the number of images per class within the dataset, which makes it difficult to classify lesion image attributes accurately.
The study highlights the potential of Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) in the early detection of skin cancer, supporting more efficient treatment and preventing advanced cases.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a significant class imbalance in the ISIC 2018 dataset (e.g., Tumor Type 5 has 6705 images while Tumor Type 4 has only 115), which limits the accuracy of classification for minority classes.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (p-values or confidence intervals); results are presented solely as accuracy rates and Mean Squared Error.

No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated only on the ISIC 2018 dataset using a 70/30 train-test split, raising concerns about generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The specific histological labels for the 7 tumor classes are not explicitly defined in the text, relying on generic 'Tumor Type' labels, which obscures clinical interpretability.
No data augmentation techniques were employed during training, relying instead on pre-processing steps (cropping, thinning, normalization) which may limit the model's robustness to variations.

The study compares the proposed hybrid method against other ANN configurations but does not benchmark against state-of-the-art deep learning models (e.g., CNNs) on the same dataset, limiting the assessment of its relative performance.
The preprocessing steps (hair removal, thinning) are manual or algorithmic heuristics that may introduce bias or fail on images with complex artifacts not handled by the median filter.
